prompt: Red-team this: an activator of C1orf116 for high LDL cholesterol

bioskeptic.reply:
Here's what the red-team turned up on **activating C1orf116 (SARG) for high LDL cholesterol** — and it's a pretty stark picture for such an obscure target.

**The genetics simply isn't there.** Every human-genetics check — direction-of-effect, causal-gene-at-locus, cis-MR, dosage sensitivity — came back *not applicable*, which means there's no genetic signal tying C1orf116 to cholesterol at all. LDL is one of the most heavily GWAS-mapped traits we have (LDLR, PCSK9, APOB, HMGCR, NPC1L1…), so a genuine LDL gene rarely stays invisible. With no genetic anchor, the whole target rationale is resting on something none of these sources can see. I weigh this as the single most serious concern.

**The protein isn't where LDL is set.** C1orf116 sits below 1 TPM in liver (0.22) and in every arterial tissue, and at single-cell resolution it's confined to squamous/apical epithelia — esophagus, skin, eye — not hepatocytes, enterocytes, or macrophages. LDL is overwhelmingly a liver-clearance trait. Normally I'd discount the expression check because a drug can act on a different organ than the diseased one, but here the disease organ *is* the expected site of action and the target still isn't made there — so that usual escape hatch doesn't apply. This is my second big one, and it dovetails with the genetics.

**Supporting flags:** the **mouse knockout** has phenotypes in kidney, retina, ear, and lens — nothing lipid-related (though note that's a loss-of-function readout and you're proposing to *activate*, so it's an imperfect mirror). And the **literature is empty**: SARG's known biology is as an androgen-regulated, EMT-associated epithelial protein, and the handful of "lipid" hits I found were botanical false matches (Sargassum algae, the "Sarg." plant-author abbreviation), not the gene.

**One practical gap:** there's no drug attached, and C1orf116 has no ChEMBL entry, no known ligand, and no solved structure — so even setting aside biology, whether you *can* pharmacologically activate it, and by what mechanism raising it would lower LDL, is entirely unestablished.

If you can point me to whatever put C1orf116 on your radar — a screen hit, a proteomics signal, a preprint — I can dig into that specific thread; right now the convergence of no genetics, no relevant expression, and no literature is what stands out most.

flagged.name: not_expressed_in_tissue
flagged.title: Expressed in the affected tissue
flagged.what_it_checks: A drug can only act where its target is actually present. This checks whether the target is switched on in the tissue the disease affects. If the target isn't made there, there is nothing in that tissue for the drug to act on.
flagged.finding: C1orf116 is not expressed in the tissue affected by Hypercholesterolemia (Liver 0.22 TPM, Artery_Aorta 0.09 TPM, Artery_Coronary 0.08 TPM, Artery_Tibial 0.09 TPM; below 1 TPM), nor in its driver cell type — its RNA is highest in Esophagus_Mucosa (126), Skin_Sun_Exposed_Lower_leg (58), Lung (55). A real absence, but weigh it against the drug's actual site of action (a drug can act on a different organ, e.g. a diuretic on the kidney).
flagged.reliability: Two common false alarms: (1) the drug deliberately acts on a DIFFERENT organ than the diseased one (for example, a water pill acts on the kidney to treat the heart); (2) the target's working cells are a tiny fraction of the tissue (like specific neurons), so a bulk measurement reads near-zero even though the target is there.
flagged.blind_spots: FP classes: (1) site-of-action != disease-organ (loop diuretics act on SLC12A1/NKCC2 in kidney medulla to treat cardiac/hepatic edema); (2) cell-type-diluted targets (SLC6A4/SERT, OPRK1) read <1 TPM in bulk; (3) inducible enzymes (PTGS2/COX-2). The single-cell rescue only tames class 2. Bulk-only precision ceiling ~0.67.
flagged.benchmark: On the test set it catches about 41% of wrong claims and false-alarms on about 17% of real drugs — right roughly 3 out of 4 times it fires.

flagged.name: absent_from_driver_cell
flagged.title: Present in the disease's driver cell
flagged.what_it_checks: A tissue is a mixture of many cell types, and usually one specific cell type drives the disease. Even if the target seems present in the tissue as a whole, this checks whether it is present in that particular disease-driving cell. If not, the drug can't act where it actually matters.
flagged.finding: C1orf116 is not expressed in the cell type that drives Hypercholesterolemia — its single-cell expression is confined to unrelated cell types (highest in Esophageal apical cells (749), Apical squamous epithelial cells (749), Ocular epithelial cells (380)) — so even where the disease acts, the driving cells lack the target for the drug to engage.
flagged.reliability: High-recall but noisy — same trap as the tissue check: 'absent from the driving cell' can't tell apart a target that works through a different route or a rare cell from one that's truly irrelevant; and the single-cell data doesn't resolve every cell type well.
flagged.blind_spots: Same expression confound as #7; HPA's single-cell atlas has limited resolution for some driver cells (e.g. raphe serotonergic neurons for depression). Precision ~0.66, recall ~0.76 — the high-recall dial of the expression axis.
flagged.benchmark: Catches the most wrong claims on the test set (about 78%) and is the best at catching disproven genetic links — but it false-alarms on about 56% of real drugs, so it is right only about two-thirds of the times it fires.

flagged.name: mouse_ko_normal
flagged.title: Mouse-knockout phenotype
flagged.what_it_checks: If a target is truly needed for a disease, then completely removing that gene in an animal should cause a related problem. This checks whether deleting the gene in mice produces any effect relevant to the disease. If the mouse is essentially fine, the target may not be required after all.
flagged.finding: The mouse knockout of C1orf116 produces no phenotype relevant to Hypercholesterolemia — its 6 knockout phenotype(s) are in unrelated systems (e.g. small kidney, abnormal auditory brainstem response, abnormal retina morphology, cataract) — so removing the target does not produce the expected disease effect, suggesting it is not required for the disease.
flagged.reliability: Noisy because a mouse is not a human: the human disease may not show up in mice, a backup gene may cover for the missing one, or a drug may work differently than fully deleting the gene.
flagged.blind_spots: ~90% of FPs are irreducible mouse!=human (viable KO whose adult phenotype doesn't match the human disease). OT aggregates phenotypes across alleles, so a lethality label can co-occur with rich adult data. Paralog compensation and drug!=full-KO also cause FPs.
flagged.benchmark: On the test set it catches about 66% of wrong claims and false-alarms on about 35% of real drugs — right roughly 7 out of 10 times it fires.

concerns.title: No genetic or causal evidence linking C1orf116 to LDL cholesterol
concerns.explanation: The genetic-axis checks (direction-of-effect, causal-gene-at-locus, cis-MR, dosage sensitivity) all came back "not applicable" — meaning there is no human genetic signal tying C1orf116 to cholesterol at all. GWAS of LDL/lipids repeatedly implicate LDLR, PCSK9, APOB, HMGCR, NPC1L1, etc., and C1orf116 is not among them. Without any genetic anchor, the entire rationale for the target rests on something outside these databases.
concerns.basis: genetic-axis checks all N/A; no cis-MR or direction-of-effect evidence

concerns.title: Target barely expressed in liver, artery, or any lipid-relevant cell
concerns.explanation: C1orf116 RNA is below 1 TPM in liver (0.22) and all arterial tissues, and its single-cell expression is confined to squamous/apical epithelia (esophagus, skin, ocular) — not hepatocytes, enterocytes, or macrophages, the cells that govern LDL handling. LDL cholesterol is overwhelmingly a liver-driven trait (LDLR-mediated clearance). If the target isn't made in the tissue that sets LDL, there is little for an activator to engage there. The expression checks are noisy in general (site-of-action can differ from disease organ), but here the disease organ IS the expected site and the target is still absent — so the false-alarm escape hatches don't really apply.
concerns.basis: not_expressed_in_tissue + absent_from_driver_cell; GTEx/HPA; disease-organ = expected site of action

concerns.title: Mouse knockout shows no lipid phenotype
concerns.explanation: Deleting C1orf116 in mice produces phenotypes in unrelated systems (small kidney, retinal/auditory/cataract), with nothing touching cholesterol or lipoprotein metabolism. This is a knockout (loss of function), and the proposal is to activate the gene, so it's not a perfect mirror — but if the gene were a meaningful node in cholesterol handling you'd typically expect some lipid readout on removal. Combined with the expression and genetic gaps, it reinforces that the target's normal biology doesn't obviously touch LDL.
concerns.basis: mouse_ko_normal (precision ~0.70); KO vs activation is imperfect mirror

concerns.title: Essentially no published biology connecting C1orf116/SARG to cholesterol
concerns.explanation: C1orf116 (aka SARG, "specifically androgen-regulated gene") has ~500 PubMed hits under its aliases, but a targeted search combining it with cholesterol/lipid/LDL returns only ~26, and on inspection those are false matches (Sargassum algae, Pinus roxburghii Sarg., hawthorn Crataegus...Sarg. — the "Sarg." botanical author abbreviation, not the gene). Its documented role is as an androgen-regulated, epithelial/mesenchymal-transition-associated protein — no mechanistic tie to lipoprotein metabolism. So beyond the databases, there's no literature basis for the LDL hypothesis either.
concerns.basis: PubMed: no genuine C1orf116-cholesterol papers; lipid hits are botanical "Sarg." false matches

concerns.title: No drug named — direction and druggability unverifiable
concerns.explanation: There's no compound attached to this claim and C1orf116 has no ChEMBL target record, no known small-molecule ligand, and an empty PDB (no solved structure) — SARG is a poorly characterized intracellular protein without an obvious ligandable pocket. "Activating" it is easy to state but there's no evidence anyone can actually do it pharmacologically, and no mechanism by which raising it would lower LDL. This is a reasoning-based flag, not a database hit.
concerns.basis: reasoning: no ChEMBL target id, no PDB structure, no known activator; druggability of activation unclear

claude.reply:
## Where this falls apart

**1. There is no genetic anchor, and that's disqualifying.** LDL is the most heavily GWAS'd trait in existence; C1orf116 does not show up in it. Its formal annotations describe an androgen-regulated ORF mapped to 1q32 with an androgen response element in intron 1, cloned from LNCaP cells, with disease links to prostate/lymph-node carcinoma and Bardet-Biedl 16 — not lipids. **[grounded: OMIM 611680, GeneCards]** Absent a human allelic series, you have no direction of effect: nothing says more C1orf116 means less LDL. **[reasoning]**

**2. Wrong tissue for the disease.** Plasma LDL is set almost entirely by hepatocyte LDLR flux. C1orf116/SARG is a prostate-enriched, androgen-inducible cytoplasmic protein studied as an epithelial/EMT gene. **[grounded: Proteintech/HPA; AACR Abstract 853]** A liver-relevant mechanism has to be invented from scratch, and a systemic activator will hit the tissues where it's actually abundant. **[reasoning]**

**3. "Activator" is doing enormous work.** It's a ~601-aa protein of largely unknown function with two splice isoforms **[grounded: Proteintech]**, no enzymatic activity, no described ligand pocket, no assayable activity state. You cannot screen for agonism of a protein whose functional readout doesn't exist. This is really a stabilizer/expression program (glue, ASO, CRISPRa) wearing a small-molecule costume. **[reasoning]**

**4. Androgen biology poisons the well.** Anything that raises an AR-driven, EMT-implicated gene proposed as a driver of epithelial phenotype in cancer carries proliferative risk in prostate epithelium, plus sex-dimorphic PD. **[grounded: AACR Abstract 853 + reasoning]**

**5. The efficacy bar is inhuman.** Generic statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 mAbs, inclisiran, bempedoic acid, and oral PCSK9 agents already occupy the space. A first-in-class with an unknown mechanism needs either large incremental LDL lowering on top of maximal therapy or a population the incumbents can't serve. **[reasoning]**

**6. Most likely provenance: a co-expression/ML artifact** — C1orf116 sits in androgen and epithelial modules that correlate with lipid traits through sex and adiposity confounding. Correlation is being read as causal, in an untested direction. **[reasoning]**
claude.label: Claude
